1:
On Revelation 5:1 — “And I saw in the right hand of him that sat on the throne a book written within and on the backside, sealed with seven seals” — the witnesses mark the sealed book in God’s hand.

The Latin fathers read it of Scripture. Victorinus: this book signifies the Old Testament, given into the hands of Christ. Bede: holy Scripture laid open through the Incarnation, the Old Testament without, the New within. The grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott: a roll lying on the open palm (epi), written within and on the back, fast sealed with seven seals.

The verse shows the sealed book. In God’s right hand lies a scroll, written on both sides, sealed with seven seals. The hidden counsels of God await an opener.

So the witnesses see, in the hand of the Enthroned, a mysterious sealed book. The witnesses note its position: “in the right hand of him that sat on the throne” — lying on the open palm, the right hand being the emblem of God’s sovereign power; the book is held forth, as Wesley notes, to be given to one worthy. They mark its fulness: “written within and on the backside” — covered with writing on both sides, the inside and the back, signifying that it is complete, crammed full, nothing wanting; the closely-written roll holds a vast content. They observe the security of its closing: “sealed with seven seals” — perfectly, completely sealed, its contents hidden and secured until rightfully opened. They note the range of interpretation as to what the book is: the Latin fathers (Victorinus, Bede) read it of holy Scripture, especially the Old Testament now opened through Christ; others, with most later expositors, take it as the book of God’s sealed counsels and decrees, the unfolding of his providential and judicial purposes for the world and the church, the very “things which must be hereafter.” They observe that on either reading the book contains God’s hidden will, closed to all until one worthy unseals it. They note that the sevenfold sealing makes the opening the great question of the chapter. The verse thus presents the sealed book: God’s full and secret purposes, written and sealed in his hand — for whose opening, the next verse cries, a worthy one is sought.
2:
On Revelation 5:2 — “And I saw a strong angel proclaiming with a loud voice, Who is worthy to open the book, and to loose the seals thereof?” — the witnesses mark the great challenge.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the herald. Robertson: a strong angel (Revelation 10:1) whose call could reach the universe’s limits. Barnes: heralds were chosen for strength of voice. Ellicott: “worthy” implies moral fitness (Romans 1:4), not mere strength. Adam Clarke: who can open its mysteries?

The verse sounds the challenge. A strong angel asks who is worthy to open the book. The question searches all creation for a fit opener.

So the witnesses see the challenge of the sealed book proclaimed throughout the universe. The witnesses note the herald: “a strong angel” — one of mighty power, whose loud voice could carry to the farthest bounds of creation; the magnitude of the question required a herald of corresponding strength. They mark the proclamation: “with a loud voice” — a public, universal summons, that every creature in heaven, earth, and under the earth might hear. They observe the searching question: “Who is worthy to open the book, and to loose the seals thereof?” — not merely who is strong enough, but, as Ellicott insists, who is morally worthy, who has the fitness and right to break the seals and disclose God’s hidden counsels. They note that the question is a quest: it goes out through the whole universe seeking one qualified to take and open the book — to unveil and execute the purposes of God. They observe that the emphasis on “worthy” lifts the matter above mere ability: the opener must have a dignity and right that fits him to handle the secret will of God. They note the suspense the question creates: the whole creation is challenged, and the answer is not yet given. The verse thus raises the great challenge: who is worthy to open the sealed book of God? — a question to which, the next verse declares, no creature could respond.

So the witnesses see the challenge of the sealed book proclaimed throughout the universe. The herald “a strong angel” is one of mighty power, whose loud voice could carry to the farthest bounds of creation; the magnitude of the question required a herald of corresponding strength. The proclamation “with a loud voice” is a public, universal summons, that every creature in heaven, earth, and under the earth might hear. The searching question, “Who is worthy to open the book, and to loose the seals?” asks not merely who is strong enough, but (Ellicott insists) who is morally worthy, with the fitness and right to disclose God’s hidden counsels. The question is a quest through the whole creation, and the answer is not yet given.
3:
On Revelation 5:3 — “And no man in heaven, nor in earth, neither under the earth, was able to open the book, neither to look thereon” — the witnesses mark the failure of all creation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the universal scope. Robertson: heaven, earth, under the earth — the whole universe (Philippians 2:10). Barnes: no one, not limited to man; angels cannot penetrate God’s decrees. Adam Clarke: neither angels, men, nor devils can fathom them. Wesley: none in the universe, no, not Mary herself.

The verse records the universal failure. No one in heaven, earth, or under the earth could open or even look on the book. All creation is found unworthy.

So the witnesses see the angel’s challenge meet with silence: no creature anywhere could answer it. The witnesses note the comprehensive sweep: “no man in heaven, nor in earth, neither under the earth” — better, no one, for it is not limited to mankind; the three regions name the whole created universe — the angels and saints in heaven, all the living on earth, and the dead beneath. They mark the total incapacity: none in all this range “was able to open the book, neither to look thereon” — not one was found worthy even to take the book and gaze into it, let alone break its seals. They observe the expositors’ force: the highest angels cannot penetrate the sealed decrees of God; no man, however wise, no departed spirit, no power above or below, can fathom or unfold his hidden counsels; as Clarke says, neither angels, men, nor devils can reach them. They note Wesley’s pointed addition that none in the universe could — “no, not Mary herself.” They observe the purpose of this universal failure: it magnifies the worthiness of the one who can; by showing that no creature is fit, it prepares the way for the unique, more-than-creaturely worthiness of the Lamb. They note the pathos of the moment: the whole creation challenged and found wanting. The verse thus records the failure of all created things: none in heaven, earth, or under the earth could open or even look on the book — a deadlock that draws, in the next verse, the seer’s tears.
4:
On Revelation 5:4 — “And I wept much, because no man was found worthy to open and to read the book, neither to look thereon” — the witnesses mark the seer’s tears.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the weeping. Robertson: “I kept on weeping much” (eklaion poly, imperfect). Adam Clarke: because the world and the Church might be deprived of the book’s knowledge. Ellicott: the emphatic “I”; not a failure of faith but tears at the seeming loss. Wesley: a weeping which sprang from greatness of mind; the Revelation was not written without tears.

The verse records John’s grief. He wept much that none was found worthy to open the book. The hidden counsels seemed lost to view.

So the witnesses see the seer break into tears at the universal failure to open the book. The witnesses note the depth of his grief: “I wept much” — the imperfect tense, as Robertson observes, pictures continued, even loud weeping; John gave way to a flood of emotion. They mark the cause: “because no man was found worthy to open and to read the book, neither to look thereon” — his sorrow was that the precious contents, the unveiling of God’s purposes for the world and the church, seemed about to remain forever sealed and unknown; the book that promised to disclose the future appeared closed to all. They observe Clarke’s sense that John wept because the world and the church might be deprived of the book’s knowledge. They note Ellicott’s and Wesley’s point that this was no failure of faith but a tender, large-hearted grief; Wesley adds that the Revelation was not written without tears, nor will it be understood without them. They observe the emphatic “I” the expositors mark: the seer is not ashamed to record his own weeping. They note the human poignancy: the longing to know God’s will frustrated by the absence of a worthy opener. They observe that the tears set off, by contrast, the joy of the discovery to come. The verse thus records John’s much weeping over the unopened book — a grief the next verse answers with the announcement of the prevailing Lion.
5:
On Revelation 5:5 — “And one of the elders saith unto me, Weep not: behold, the Lion of the tribe of Juda, the Root of David, hath prevailed to open the book” — the witnesses mark the announcement of the prevailing Lion.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the titles. Adam Clarke: the Lion of Judah, Jesus Christ sprung from that tribe, alluding to Genesis 49:9. Ellicott: he “conquered” so as to open the roll. The Eastern fathers Cyril: the Only-begotten Son. Wesley: the Lion of Judah, who has overcome.

The verse answers the tears. An elder bids John weep not, for the Lion of Judah has prevailed to open the book. Christ’s victory unseals God’s counsels.

So the witnesses see the seer’s grief answered by the announcement of one who has prevailed. The witnesses note the comfort: “one of the elders saith unto me, Weep not” — a representative of the redeemed church stays John’s tears; the deadlock is broken, there is one worthy after all. They mark the first title: “the Lion of the tribe of Juda” — drawn from Jacob’s blessing on Judah, the lion’s whelp; Christ, sprung from Judah, is the royal Lion, mighty and victorious, the promised king of Judah’s line. They observe the second: “the Root of David” — both the descendant of David (his shoot) and, more deeply, the root from which David himself sprang, the messianic king who is both David’s son and David’s Lord. They note the decisive word: “hath prevailed” (or conquered) “to open the book” — Christ has overcome, by his life, death, and resurrection, and so has won the right and power to open the sealed book; his victory is the ground of his worthiness. They observe Cyril’s confession of the Only-begotten Son. They note the contrast of the imagery to come: announced as a Lion of conquering might, he will appear (next verse) as a slain Lamb — the victory won through sacrifice. They observe that the elder’s word turns mourning to expectation. The verse thus answers the tears: the Lion of Judah, the Root of David, has prevailed to open the book — the conqueror whom John now beholds, in the next verse, as the Lamb.
6:
On Revelation 5:6 — “And lo, in the midst of the throne… stood a Lamb as it had been slain, having seven horns and seven eyes, which are the seven Spirits of God” — the witnesses mark the slain yet living Lamb.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the paradox. Adam Clarke: a Lamb (arnion, a little lamb), as if now in the act of being offered. Ellicott: standing as if having been slain. Wesley: he stands — he lieth no more; the seven horns full power, the seven eyes all-seeing Spirit. The Puritan Henry: the book taken to be opened by him.

The verse unveils the Lamb. In the midst of the throne stands a Lamb as slain, with seven horns and seven eyes. The conquering Lion appears as the sacrificed Lamb.

So the witnesses see the announced Lion appear, astonishingly, as a slain Lamb. The witnesses note the great paradox: the elder spoke of a Lion, but John beholds “a Lamb as it had been slain” — the symbol of meekness and sacrifice, bearing the marks of slaughter, yet alive and standing; the conquering king is the crucified, risen victim. They mark the position: “in the midst of the throne” — central, sharing the very throne of God, the Lamb in the closest place of divine majesty. They observe the marks of sacrifice: “as it had been slain” — as Wesley says, he stands, he lies no more, yet bears forever the tokens of his death; the slain Lamb lives and reigns. They note the emblems of power and knowledge: “seven horns” — horns the symbol of strength, seven of perfect, complete power and dominion; and “seven eyes, which are the seven Spirits of God” — the fulness of the Spirit, perfect all-seeing wisdom and omniscience, sent forth into all the earth. They observe the profound theology: the Lamb is at once the sacrificed Redeemer and the omnipotent, omniscient Lord, sharing God’s throne and Spirit. They note that the victory announced as a Lion’s was won by the Lamb’s death; might triumphs through sacrifice. They observe that this is the central figure of the book. The verse thus unveils the slain yet living Lamb, all-powerful and all-seeing, in the midst of the throne — who, the next verse declares, takes the book.
7:
On Revelation 5:7 — “And he came and took the book out of the right hand of him that sat upon the throne” — the witnesses mark the Lamb taking the book.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the act. Robertson: “he taketh” (eilephen, perfect), a vivid dramatic picture, “he has taken it.” Adam Clarke: with Jesus alone are all the counsels of God (John 1:18). Wesley: here was “Ask of me” (Psalm 2:8) fulfilled gloriously; he took it.

The verse records the great act. The Lamb came and took the book from God’s right hand. The worthy one receives the sealed counsels of God.

So the witnesses see the Lamb step forward and take the book — the decisive act of the chapter. The witnesses note the approach: “he came” — the Lamb drew near to the throne, approaching the very seat of God, which (as Gill observes) no mere creature without a mediator could do. They mark the act itself: “and took the book out of the right hand of him that sat upon the throne” — the Lamb received the sealed scroll from God’s sovereign hand; the perfect tense, Robertson notes, gives a vivid, dramatic vividness, “he has taken it.” They observe the meaning the expositors draw: the worthy one alone is fit to receive God’s sealed counsels; with Jesus alone, as Clarke says, are all the mysteries of God, and to him the Father commits the unfolding of his will. They note Wesley’s sense that here the promise of Psalm 2, “Ask of me, and I shall give thee,” is gloriously fulfilled: the Father gives into the Son’s hand the book of his purposes, the government of the future. They observe that the act marks the Lamb’s investiture with authority over the destinies the book contains; to hold the book is to hold the right to execute God’s plan. They note the quiet majesty of the scene: no struggle, but the simple taking of what is his by right of his victory. They observe that all heaven, seeing it, breaks into worship. The verse thus records the central act: the Lamb takes the book from the hand of God — at which, the next verse says, the living creatures and elders fall down before him.
8:
On Revelation 5:8 — “And when he had taken the book, the four beasts and four and twenty elders fell down before the Lamb, having every one… golden vials full of odours, which are the prayers of saints” — the witnesses mark heaven’s worship of the Lamb.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the homage. Robertson: when “he took” the book; the harps and golden bowls of incense, the prayers of the saints. Adam Clarke: the whole Church acknowledges Christ alone worthy. The Puritan Henry: the vials full of odours, the prayers of the saints. Wesley: now homage is done to the Lamb by every creature.

The verse opens the worship of the Lamb. The living creatures and elders fall before him with harps and incense of the saints’ prayers. The taking of the book draws forth adoration.

So the witnesses see the Lamb’s taking of the book greeted at once with heaven’s worship. The witnesses note the homage: “the four beasts and four and twenty elders fell down before the Lamb” — the living creatures and the redeemed elders, who had worshipped the Enthroned (ch. 4), now prostrate themselves before the Lamb; the same adoration given to God is given to the Lamb — a testimony to his deity. They mark the instruments of praise: “having every one of them harps” — harps, the instruments of joyful, melodious praise, for the song they are about to sing. They observe the golden bowls: “golden vials full of odours, which are the prayers of saints” — golden censers brimming with fragrant incense, expressly interpreted as the prayers of God’s people; the prayers of the saints rise before the throne like sweet incense, precious and accepted. They note the tender truth the expositors find here: the prayers of the lowliest believers on earth are gathered in golden bowls in heaven, fragrant before God and the Lamb — not one is lost. They observe that worship of the Lamb and the offering of the saints’ prayers go together. They note that the whole church, represented by the elders, joins the living creatures in adoring the Redeemer. The verse thus opens the worship of the Lamb: the creatures and elders fall before him with harps and the incense of the saints’ prayers — and sing, in the next verse, the new song of redemption.
9:
On Revelation 5:9 — “And they sung a new song, saying, Thou art worthy… for thou wast slain, and hast redeemed us to God by thy blood out of every kindred, and tongue, and people, and nation” — the witnesses mark the new song of redemption.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the song. Robertson: “a new song” (oden kainen), consequent on redemption; cf. Revelation 14:3. Adam Clarke: a new and most excellent song, composed on the blessings of the Gospel. Barnes: distinguished from songs sung before redemption was consummated. Wesley: the living creatures also among the redeemed.

The verse gives the new song. The Lamb is worthy because he was slain and redeemed men of every nation by his blood. Redemption is the ground of the Lamb’s praise.

So the witnesses see heaven break into a new song, the song of redemption, before the Lamb. The witnesses note its newness: “a new song” — new because it celebrates a new and finished work, redemption accomplished; a song that could not be sung until the Lamb was slain and had triumphed, distinct from all praise that went before. They mark the ascription of worthiness: “Thou art worthy… to take the book, and to open the seals thereof” — the very worthiness the angel had sought is now joyfully owned in the Lamb. They observe the ground, which differs from chapter 4’s: there God was worthy as Creator; here the Lamb is worthy “for thou wast slain” — his worthiness rests on his sacrificial death. They note the great work celebrated: “and hast redeemed us to God by thy blood” — by the price of his own blood he purchased a people for God, ransoming them from sin and bondage. They observe the universal scope: “out of every kindred, and tongue, and people, and nation” — the redeemed are gathered from all races and languages of mankind; the Lamb’s blood knows no national bounds. They note that the redeemed sing of their own redemption, owning themselves bought by his blood. They observe that this song of the Redeemer answers and crowns the earlier song of the Creator. The verse thus lifts the new song: the Lamb is worthy, for he was slain and redeemed a people of every nation by his blood — whom, the next verse adds, he made kings and priests.
10:
On Revelation 5:10 — “And hast made us unto our God kings and priests: and we shall reign on the earth” — the witnesses mark the dignity of the redeemed.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the honour. Robertson: “a kingdom and priests” (Revelation 1:6; Exodus 19:6); “they reign” (futuristic present). Adam Clarke refers to 1 Peter 2:9. Wesley: they speak of themselves in the third person out of deep self-abasement; they shall reign over the earth.

The verse names the redeemed’s estate. The Lamb made them kings and priests, and they shall reign. Redemption raises sinners to royal-priestly dignity.

So the witnesses see the new song proceed to the high estate redemption confers. The witnesses note the dignity bestowed: “hast made us unto our God kings and priests” — the redeemed are raised to a royal-priesthood, drawing on the ancient promise to Israel that they should be a kingdom of priests; in Christ believers are made kings, sharing his dominion, and priests, with access to God and the offering of spiritual sacrifices. They mark that this honour is “unto our God” — their kingship and priesthood are exercised toward and for God, in his service. They observe the future reign: “and we shall reign on the earth” — the redeemed are destined to reign, the expositors variously referring it to the present spiritual reign, the future reign of the saints, or the new earth. They note Wesley’s tender observation that the redeemed here speak of themselves in the third person (“them… they” in the best texts) out of deep self-abasement, owning the grace that raised them. They observe the wonder of the transformation the song celebrates: those ransomed by blood from every nation are not merely pardoned but exalted to royalty and priesthood, destined to reign. They note that this answers the crowns and thrones of the elders seen in chapter 4. The verse thus crowns the new song with the redeemed’s dignity: made kings and priests to God, they shall reign on the earth — a song the next verse swells with the voice of myriad angels.
11:
On Revelation 5:11 — “And I beheld, and I heard the voice of many angels round about the throne… and the number of them was ten thousand times ten thousand, and thousands of thousands” — the witnesses mark the innumerable angelic chorus.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the host. Robertson: a new feature, the outer, vaster circle of angels in antiphonal singing. Adam Clarke: “myriads of myriads and chiliads of chiliads,” an innumerable multitude, in reference to Daniel 7:10. Wesley: men are represented through this vision as nearer to God than any angels.

The verse widens the chorus to the angels. A countless host of angels surrounds the throne and the redeemed. The whole heavenly world joins the praise.

So the witnesses see the worship widen from the redeemed to the innumerable angels. The witnesses note the new and vaster circle: “the voice of many angels round about the throne, and the beasts, and the elders” — beyond the inner ring of living creatures and elders, a great outer circle of angels takes up the praise; heaven’s worship spreads in widening rings. They mark the staggering number: “ten thousand times ten thousand, and thousands of thousands” — myriads of myriads and thousands of thousands, an uncountable multitude, echoing Daniel’s vision of the thousand thousands that ministered before the Ancient of Days. They observe the antiphonal pattern the expositors note: the angels answer and amplify the new song of the redeemed; the worship rises in responsive waves through the ranks of heaven. They note Wesley’s striking observation that throughout this vision the redeemed (the elders) are placed nearer to God than the angels — the ransomed sinners in the inner circle, the unfallen angels in the outer; redemption gives a place even the angels do not share. They observe that the angels, though not themselves redeemed, rejoice in the redemption of men and join to honour the Lamb. They note the magnitude: all heaven’s countless host unites in the praise. The verse thus swells the chorus with the innumerable angels around the throne — whose own ascription of worthiness the next verse records.
12:
On Revelation 5:12 — “Saying with a loud voice, Worthy is the Lamb that was slain to receive power, and riches, and wisdom, and strength, and honour, and glory, and blessing” — the witnesses mark the sevenfold doxology to the Lamb.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the ascription. Robertson: the same point as the song of verses 9–10, in the third person now. Adam Clarke: power, riches, wisdom, strength, honour, glory, blessing — all ascribed to the slain Lamb. Ellicott: the sevenfold doxology of the angels. Wesley: this sevenfold applause answers the seven seals.

The verse gives the angels’ doxology. Worthy is the slain Lamb to receive a sevenfold tribute of power and praise. Heaven ascribes all glory to the Redeemer.

So the witnesses see the angelic host cry aloud the worthiness of the slain Lamb in a sevenfold ascription. The witnesses note the loud unison: “saying with a loud voice” — the countless angels proclaim with full voice, that all may hear, the Lamb’s desert. They mark the ground, the same as the redeemed’s song: “Worthy is the Lamb that was slain” — his slaughter, his sacrificial death, is the reason for the praise; the angels, though not redeemed, own that the slain Lamb deserves all honour. They observe the sevenfold tribute: “power, and riches, and wisdom, and strength, and honour, and glory, and blessing” — seven attributes and ascriptions, the number of completeness, heaping the fulness of all praise upon him; Clarke draws out each as omnipotence, beneficence, omniscience, prevailing might, reputation, splendour, and thanksgiving. They note the difference from the elders’ song: the elders said “worthy art thou,” addressing the Lamb directly; the angels say “worthy is the Lamb,” in the third person, for they are (as Wesley notes) less nearly related to him than the redeemed. They observe Wesley’s point that this sevenfold applause answers the seven seals. They note the completeness: the whole compass of glory is ascribed to the Lamb. They observe that the angels join the redeemed in crowning the Lamb with all honour. The verse thus lifts the sevenfold doxology: worthy is the slain Lamb to receive all power and praise — an ascription the next verse widens to every creature.
13:
On Revelation 5:13 — “And every creature which is in heaven, and on the earth… heard I saying, Blessing, and honour, and glory, and power, be unto him that sitteth upon the throne, and unto the Lamb for ever and ever” — the witnesses mark the universal chorus.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the widening circle. Robertson: every created thing (pan ktisma) from all four fields of life. Adam Clarke: all creation, by personification, gives praise to the Lamb. Ellicott: the third chorus, the chorus of the universe. Wesley: the fourfold word of praise agrees with the four regions of the world.

The verse swells to the whole creation. Every creature ascribes blessing and honour to God and the Lamb forever. All that exists joins the worship.

So the witnesses see the worship reach its widest sweep: the whole of creation joins. The witnesses note the universal compass: “every creature which is in heaven, and on the earth, and under the earth, and such as are in the sea” — the four great regions of the universe, every created thing without exception, takes up the praise; the circles of worship have widened from the living creatures, to the elders, to the angels, and now to all creation. They mark the ascription: “Blessing, and honour, and glory, and power” — a fourfold tribute, which Wesley observes agrees with the four regions of the world; the whole creation renders its homage. They observe the joint object: “unto him that sitteth upon the throne, and unto the Lamb” — God and the Lamb are praised together, on equal terms, the same worship given to both — a clear testimony to the Lamb’s deity, that the creature’s adoration is rendered to him with the Father. They note the duration: “for ever and ever” — the praise is everlasting, never to cease. They observe that this is, as Ellicott calls it, the chorus of the universe: the song begun by the redeemed and swelled by the angels now becomes the voice of all created things. They note the grandeur of the vision: every being that exists unites to glorify God and the Lamb. The verse thus lifts the universal chorus: all creation ascribes blessing, honour, glory, and power to God and the Lamb forever — sealed, in the next verse, by the creatures’ Amen.
14:
On Revelation 5:14 — “And the four beasts said, Amen. And the four and twenty elders fell down and worshipped him that liveth for ever and ever” — the witnesses mark the sealing of the worship.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the close. Robertson: the four living creatures give their approval, “Amen”; the elders fall down in silent adoration. Adam Clarke: they acknowledge that what is attributed to Christ is his due. Barnes: the living creatures and elders began the praise and now close it. The Eastern fathers Cyril appears here.

The verse seals the worship. The living creatures say Amen, and the elders fall down and worship. Heaven’s adoration ends where it began, in prostrate praise.

So the witnesses see the great chorus brought to its close and seal. The witnesses note the confirming “Amen”: “the four beasts said, Amen” — the four living creatures, who had begun the praise (ch. 4) and led the worship, now set their seal of assent to the universal ascription; “Amen” affirms that all the creation has said is true and worthy. They mark the final act: “the four and twenty elders fell down and worshipped him that liveth for ever and ever” — the redeemed elders close the whole service, as they had opened it, with silent, prostrate adoration before the eternal God and the Lamb. They observe the fitting symmetry the expositors note: the living creatures and elders began the heavenly worship, and they close it; the song that swelled outward through angels and all creation returns at last to the inner circle’s wordless worship. They note that the “Amen” and the prostration are the proper response to such a vision: assent and adoration. They observe that the worship ends not in noise but in the deepest reverence, falling down before him who lives forever. They note that the whole chapter has moved from a sealed book and a weeping seer to the universal, sealed worship of God and the Lamb — the Redeemer found worthy. They observe that this closes the throne-and-Lamb vision and prepares for the opening of the seals. The verse thus seals heaven’s worship: the creatures’ Amen and the elders’ prostrate adoration of the eternal God and the Lamb — the worthy one now ready to open the book.
